activity_metadata.objective: Students will analyze how the author uses dialogue between Papa and Mama Logan to convey themes about family relationships and responses to conflict in Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the "Your Turn" segment of the Roll of Thunder, Hear Me Cry, Theme Skills lesson.
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity supports the end-of-unit argumentative essay "How do relationships shape us?" by developing students' ability to analyze how authors use specific textual details to reveal themes about relationships. Students practice identifying how dialogue reveals character values and family dynamics, skills they will need when analyzing how relationships between characters in unit texts demonstrate the power of human connections to influence behavior, decisions, and personal growth.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This activity connects to the essential question "How do relationships shape us?" by examining the Logan family's relationship dynamics during a moment of external threat. Students explore how Taylor uses the conversation between Papa and Mama Logan to show that strong family relationships are built on honest communication, shared decision-making, and mutual support during difficult times - directly addressing how family relationships shape individuals' responses to adversity and injustice.
activity_metadata.text_title: Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Excerpt from Chapter 9 of Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry

Paragraphs 1-8

(1) When supper was ready, I eagerly grabbed the iron bell before Christopher-John or Little Man could claim it, and ran onto the back porch to summon Papa, Mr. Morrison, and Stacey from the fields. As the three of them washed up on the back porch, Mama went to the end of the porch where Papa stood alone. "What did Mr. Jamison want?" she asked, her voice barely audible.

(2) Papa took the towel Mama handed him, but did not reply immediately. I was just inside the kitchen dipping out the butter beans. I moved closer to the window so that I could hear his answer.

(3) "Don't keep anything from me, David. If there's trouble, I want to know."

(4) Papa looked down at her. "Nothing to worry 'bout, honey ....just seems that Thurston Wallace been in town talking 'bout how he's not gonna let a few smart colored folks ruin his business. Says he's gonna put a stop to this shopping in Vicksburg. That's all."

(5) Mama sighed and stared out across the plowed field to the sloping pasture land. "I'm feeling scared, David," she said.

(6) Papa put down the towel. "Not yet, Mary. It's not time to be scared yet. They're just talking."

(7) Mama turned and faced him. "And when they stop talking?"

(8) "Then ...then maybe it'll be time. But right now, pretty lady," he said, leading her by the hand toward the kitchen door, "right now I've got better things to think about."
exit_ticket: What theme can readers infer from the dialogue between Mr. and Mrs. Logan?
